[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/A_Westerly_View_of_the_Colledges_in_Cambridge_New_England_by_Paul_Revere.jpeg/250px-A_Westerly_View_of_the_Colledges_in_Cambridge_New_England_by_Paul_Revere.jpeg)](/wiki/File:A_Westerly_View_of_the_Colledges_in_Cambridge_New_England_by_Paul_Revere.jpeg)

Engraving of [Harvard College](/wiki/Harvard_College "Harvard College") by [Paul Revere](/wiki/Paul_Revere "Paul Revere"), 1767. [Harvard University's endowment](/wiki/Harvard_University_endowment "Harvard University endowment") was valued at $53.2 billion as of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit).[[1]](#cite_note-Endowment-1)

A **financial endowment** is a legal structure for managing, and in many cases indefinitely perpetuating, a pool of financial resources (such as cash and bank deposits), [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate"), or other investments for a specific purpose according to [the will of its founders and donors](/wiki/Donor_intent "Donor intent").[[2]](#cite_note-Kenton-2) Endowments are often structured so that the inflation-adjusted [principal](/wiki/Principal_(finance) "Principal (finance)") or "corpus" value is kept intact, while a portion of the fund can be (and in some cases must be) spent each year, utilizing a prudent spending policy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/20171005_Novo_Nordisk_Fonden_kontor_Hellerup_0035_%2837067541564%29.jpg/250px-20171005_Novo_Nordisk_Fonden_kontor_Hellerup_0035_%2837067541564%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:20171005_Novo_Nordisk_Fonden_kontor_Hellerup_0035_(37067541564).jpg)

The [Novo Nordisk Foundation](/wiki/Novo_Nordisk_Foundation "Novo Nordisk Foundation"), the world's wealthiest foundation (as of 2022), is headquartered in [Hellerup](/wiki/Hellerup "Hellerup"), a [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") suburb.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/B%26M_Gates_Complex.jpg/250px-B%26M_Gates_Complex.jpg)](/wiki/File:B%26M_Gates_Complex.jpg)

[The Gates Foundation](/wiki/The_Gates_Foundation "The Gates Foundation"), the world's second wealthiest foundation (as of 2022), headquarters complex in Seattle as seen from the [Space Needle](/wiki/Space_Needle "Space Needle")

Endowments are often governed and managed either as a [nonprofit corporation](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization"), a [charitable foundation](/wiki/Charitable_foundation "Charitable foundation"), or a [private foundation](/wiki/Private_foundation "Private foundation") that, while serving a good cause, might not qualify as a public charity. In some jurisdictions, it is common for endowed funds to be established as a [trust](/wiki/Trust_(law) "Trust (law)") independent of the organizations and the causes the endowment is meant to serve. Institutions that commonly manage endowments include academic institutions (e.g., [colleges](/wiki/College "College"), [universities](/wiki/Universities "Universities"), and [private schools](/wiki/Private_school "Private school")); cultural institutions (e.g., museums, libraries, and theaters); service organizations (e.g., hospitals, retirement homes; the [Red Cross](/wiki/Red_Cross "Red Cross")); and religious organizations (e.g., churches, synagogues, mosques).

Private endowments are some of the wealthiest entities in the world, notably [private higher education endowments](/wiki/Lists_of_institutions_of_higher_education_by_endowment_size "Lists of institutions of higher education by endowment size"). [Harvard University's endowment](/wiki/Harvard_University_endowment "Harvard University endowment") (valued at $53.2 billion as of June 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit))[[3]](#cite_note-Harvard_Endowment-3) is the [largest academic endowment](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_the_United_States_by_endowment "List of colleges and universities in the United States by endowment") in the world.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) As of 31 December 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit), the [Novo Nordisk Foundation](/wiki/Novo_Nordisk_Foundation "Novo Nordisk Foundation") and the [Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation](/wiki/Bill_and_Melinda_Gates_Foundation "Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation") were the world's [wealthiest private foundations](/wiki/List_of_wealthiest_charitable_foundations "List of wealthiest charitable foundations"), with an endowment of $167 billion[[6]](#cite_note-6) and $67.3 billion,[[7]](#cite_note-FactSheet-7) respectively.

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Types")]

Most private endowments in the United States are governed by the [Uniform Prudent Management of Institutional Funds Act](/wiki/Uniform_Prudent_Management_of_Institutional_Funds_Act "Uniform Prudent Management of Institutional Funds Act") which is based in part on the concept of [donor intent](/wiki/Donor_intent "Donor intent") that helps define what restrictions are imposed on the principal and earnings of the fund. Endowments in the United States are commonly categorized in one of four ways:[[2]](#cite_note-Kenton-2)

1. Restricted endowment (also called permanent endowment). This is the type that is subject to UPMIFA in most states. The donor has stated that the gift is to be held permanently as an endowment, either for general purposes or for specific (or restricted) purposes.[[2]](#cite_note-Kenton-2)[[8]](#cite_note-newman-8)
2. Quasi endowment funds are designated endowments by an organization's governing body rather than by the donor. Therefore, both the principal and the income may be accessed at the organization's discretion. Quasi endowment funds are still subject to any other donor restrictions or intent.[[2]](#cite_note-Kenton-2)[[9]](#cite_note-9)
3. Term endowment is created with a gift or funds set aside by the board which can be spent in their entirety upon a set date or occurrence, such as the death of the donor.[[2]](#cite_note-Kenton-2)[[8]](#cite_note-newman-8)
4. Unrestricted endowments may be spent, saved, invested or distributed at the organization's discretion.[[2]](#cite_note-Kenton-2)

Restricted endowments ensure that the original principal, inflation-adjusted, is held in perpetuity and prudent spending methods should be applied in order to avoid the erosion of corpus over reasonable time frames. Restricted endowments may also facilitate additional donor requirements.

In the UK there are two types of endowment: permanent or expendable. Endowments have specific conditions attached and normally specify how income can be spent and whether this counts as restricted or unrestricted income for accounting purposes. A permanent endowment is one where the capital cannot be spent, while an expendable endowment is one which can be spent in certain circumstance, specified in the endowment document, but not otherwise.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Restrictions and donor intent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Restrictions and donor intent")]

Endowment revenue can be restricted by donors to serve many purposes. Endowed professorships or scholarships restricted to a particular subject are common; in some places, a donor could fund a trust exclusively for the support of a pet.[[11]](#cite_note-MyUser_Forbes_March_5_2015c-11)[[12]](#cite_note-MyUser_The_Wall_Street_Journal_March_5_2015c-12) Ignoring the restriction is called "[invading](/wiki/Endowment_invasion "Endowment invasion")" the endowment.[[13]](#cite_note-MyUser_Thenonprofittimes.com_March_5_2015c-13) But change of circumstance or financial duress like bankruptcy can preclude carrying out the donor's intent. A court can alter the use of restricted endowment under a doctrine called [cy-près](/wiki/Cy-pr%C3%A8s "Cy-près") meaning to find an alternative "as near as possible" to the donor's intent.[[13]](#cite_note-MyUser_Thenonprofittimes.com_March_5_2015c-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [University § Antecedents](/wiki/University#Antecedents "University"), and [waqf](/wiki/Waqf "Waqf")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/L%27Image_et_le_Pouvoir_-_Buste_cuirass%C3%A9_de_Marc_Aur%C3%A8le_ag%C3%A9_-_3.jpg/250px-L%27Image_et_le_Pouvoir_-_Buste_cuirass%C3%A9_de_Marc_Aur%C3%A8le_ag%C3%A9_-_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:L%27Image_et_le_Pouvoir_-_Buste_cuirass%C3%A9_de_Marc_Aur%C3%A8le_ag%C3%A9_-_3.jpg)

[Marcus Aurelius](/wiki/Marcus_Aurelius "Marcus Aurelius"), the Stoic Roman [emperor](/wiki/Emperor "Emperor") who created the first [endowed chair](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/endowed_chair "wiktionary:endowed chair") professorships

The earliest [endowed chairs](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/endowed_chair "wiktionary:endowed chair") were established by the [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") emperor and [Stoic](/wiki/Stoicism "Stoicism") philosopher [Marcus Aurelius](/wiki/Marcus_Aurelius "Marcus Aurelius") in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") in AD 176. Aurelius created one endowed chair for each of the major schools of [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy"): [Platonism](/wiki/Platonism "Platonism"), [Aristotelianism](/wiki/Aristotelianism "Aristotelianism"), [Stoicism](/wiki/Stoicism "Stoicism"), and [Epicureanism](/wiki/Epicureanism "Epicureanism"). Later, similar endowments were set up in some other major cities of the Roman Empire.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

[Waqf](/wiki/Waqf "Waqf") ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): وَقْف; [[ˈwɑqf]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Arabic "Help:IPA/Arabic")), also known as 'hubous' (حُبوس)[[16]](#cite_note-16) or [mortmain](/wiki/Mortmain "Mortmain") property, is a similar concept from [Islamic law](/wiki/Islamic_law "Islamic law"), which typically involves donating a building, plot of land or other assets for [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") religious or charitable purposes with no intention of reclaiming the assets.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The donated assets may be held by a [charitable trust](/wiki/Charitable_trust "Charitable trust").

The two oldest known *waqfiya* (deed) documents are from the 9th century, while a third one dates from the early 10th century, all three within the Abbasid Period. The oldest dated *waqfiya* goes back to 876 CE, concerns a multi-volume Qur'an edition and is held by the [Turkish and Islamic Arts Museum](/wiki/Turkish_and_Islamic_Arts_Museum "Turkish and Islamic Arts Museum") in [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"). A possibly older *waqfiya* is a papyrus held by the [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre") Museum in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), with no written date but considered to be from the mid-9th century.

The earliest known waqf in Egypt, founded by financial official Abū Bakr Muḥammad bin Ali al-Madhara'i in 919 (during the [Abbasid period](/wiki/Abbasid_dynasty "Abbasid dynasty")), is a pond called Birkat Ḥabash together with its surrounding orchards, whose revenue was to be used to operate a hydraulic complex and feed the poor. In India, wakfs are relatively common among Muslim communities and are regulated by the [Central Wakf Council](/wiki/Central_Wakf_Council "Central Wakf Council") and governed by Wakf Act 1995 (which superseded Wakf Act 1954).

In medieval Europe, endowments were used to establish [chantries](/wiki/Chantry "Chantry"), where masses and prayers were said for the dead.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Royal patrons endowed large foundations such as [Reading Abbey](/wiki/Reading_Abbey "Reading Abbey"), founded by [Henry I](/wiki/Henry_I_of_England "Henry I of England") after the death of his son in the [White Ship disaster](/wiki/White_Ship_disaster "White Ship disaster").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The endowed madrasas of the medieval Muslim world are thought to have inspired the establishment of endowed colleges at universities in Europe from the 13th century,[[20]](#cite_note-20) with strong similarities particularly noted between *waqfiya* and the foundation statutes of [Merton College, Oxford](/wiki/Merton_College,_Oxford "Merton College, Oxford"), the prototype of the English eleemosynary trust.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) These developed into eleemosynary corporations – charities established for the perpetual distribution of the alms or bounty of their founder, such as hospitals or colleges.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) In England, these had a [visitor](/wiki/Visitor "Visitor") – an overseer of the charity appointed by the founder to ensure their wishes were followed – as a necessity incident of their foundation.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

The earliest endowed professorship in Britain was the [Regius Professor of Medicine](/wiki/Regius_Professor_of_Medicine_(Aberdeen) "Regius Professor of Medicine (Aberdeen)") established in 1497 in Aberdeen by King [James IV of Scotland](/wiki/James_IV_of_Scotland "James IV of Scotland").[[26]](#cite_note-26) This was followed in England by the endowment of [Lady Margaret Professor of Divinity](/wiki/Lady_Margaret_Professor_of_Divinity "Lady Margaret Professor of Divinity") at Oxford and [Lady Margaret's Professor of Divinity](/wiki/Lady_Margaret%27s_Professor_of_Divinity "Lady Margaret's Professor of Divinity") at Cambridge in 1502 by [Lady Margaret Beaufort](/wiki/Lady_Margaret_Beaufort "Lady Margaret Beaufort"), Countess of Richmond and grandmother to the future king [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII").[[27]](#cite_note-Cambridge-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) Nearly 50 years later, Henry VIII established [regius professorships](/wiki/Regius_professor "Regius professor") at Oxford and Cambridge in divinity (theology), civil law, Hebrew, Greek, and physic (medicine).[[29]](#cite_note-29) Other individuals also endowed professorships, such as the [Lucasian Chair of Mathematics](/wiki/Lucasian_Chair_of_Mathematics "Lucasian Chair of Mathematics") at Cambridge, endowed in 1663 by [Henry Lucas](/wiki/Henry_Lucas_(politician) "Henry Lucas (politician)").[[30]](#cite_note-Bruen-30)

## Modern college and university endowments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern college and university endowments")]

See also: [Lists of institutions of higher education by endowment size](/wiki/Lists_of_institutions_of_higher_education_by_endowment_size "Lists of institutions of higher education by endowment size")

Academic institutions, such as colleges and universities, will frequently control an endowment fund that finances a portion of the operating or capital requirements of the institution. In addition to a general endowment fund, each university may also control a number of restricted endowments that are intended to fund specific areas within the institution. The most common examples are endowed professorships (also known as named chairs), and endowed [scholarships](/wiki/Scholarship "Scholarship") or fellowships.

In the United States, the endowment is often integral to the financial health of educational institutions. Alumni or friends of institutions sometimes contribute capital to the endowment. The use of endowment funding is strong in the United States and Canada but less commonly found outside of North America, with the exceptions of [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") and [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") universities. Endowment funds have also been created to support secondary and elementary school districts in several states in the United States.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Endowed professorships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Endowed professorships")]

An endowed [professorship](/wiki/Professor "Professor") (or **endowed chair**) is a position permanently paid for with the revenue from an endowment fund specifically set up for that purpose. To set up an endowed chair generally costs between US$1 and $5 million at major research universities.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Typically, the position is designated to be in a certain department. The donor might be allowed to name the position. Endowed professorships aid the university by providing a faculty member who does not have to be paid entirely out of the operating budget, allowing the university to either reduce its student-to-faculty ratio, a statistic used for [college rankings](/wiki/College_rankings "College rankings") and other institutional evaluations, or direct money that would otherwise have been spent on salaries toward other university needs. In addition, holding such a professorship is considered to be an honour in the academic world, and the university can use them to reward its best faculty or to recruit top professors from other institutions.[[33]](#cite_note-Cornell-33)

### Endowed faculty fellowships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Endowed faculty fellowships")]

An endowed faculty [fellow](/wiki/Fellow "Fellow") is a position permanently paid for to recruit and retain new and/or junior (and above) professors who have already demonstrated superior teaching and research. The donor might be allowed to name the faculty fellowship. A faculty fellow appointment cultivates confidence and institutional loyalty, keeping the institution competitive over hiring and retention of talents.

### Endowed scholarships and fellowships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Endowed scholarships and fellowships")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(January 2026)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

An endowed [scholarship](/wiki/Scholarship "Scholarship") is tuition (and possibly other costs) assistance that is permanently paid for by an organisation or individual with the revenue of an endowment fund specifically set up for that purpose. It can be either merit-based or need-based (the latter is only awarded to those students for whom the college expense would cause their family financial hardship) depending on university policy or donor preferences. Some universities will facilitate donors' meeting the students they are helping. The amount that must be donated to start an endowed scholarship can vary greatly.

Fellowships are similar, although they are most commonly associated with graduate students. In addition to helping with tuition, they may also include a stipend. Fellowships with a stipend may encourage students to work on a [doctorate](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate"). Frequently, teaching or working on research is a mandatory part of a fellowship.

## Charitable foundations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Charitable foundations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Ford_Foundation_HQ_jeh.JPG/250px-Ford_Foundation_HQ_jeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ford_Foundation_HQ_jeh.JPG)

[Ford Foundation Building](/wiki/Ford_Foundation_Building "Ford Foundation Building") in New York. In 2014, The [Ford Foundation](/wiki/Ford_Foundation "Ford Foundation") reported assets of US$12.4 billion and approved US$507.9 million in grants.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

See also: [Foundation (nonprofit)](/wiki/Foundation_(nonprofit) "Foundation (nonprofit)") and [List of wealthiest charitable foundations](/wiki/List_of_wealthiest_charitable_foundations "List of wealthiest charitable foundations")

A foundation (also a charitable foundation) is a category of [nonprofit organization](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization") or [charitable trust](/wiki/Charitable_trust "Charitable trust") that will typically provide funding and support for other [charitable organizations](/wiki/Charitable_organization "Charitable organization") through grants, but may engage directly in charitable activities. Foundations include public charitable foundations, such as [community foundations](/wiki/Community_foundation "Community foundation"), and [private foundations](/wiki/Private_foundation "Private foundation") which are typically endowed by an individual or family. The term foundation though may also be used by organizations not involved in public grant-making.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

## Fiduciary management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Fiduciary management")]

See also: [Uniform Prudent Management of Institutional Funds Act](/wiki/Uniform_Prudent_Management_of_Institutional_Funds_Act "Uniform Prudent Management of Institutional Funds Act")

A financial endowment is typically overseen by a board of [trustees](/wiki/Trustee "Trustee") and managed by a trustee or team of professional managers. Typically, the financial operation of the endowment is designed to achieve the stated objectives of the endowment.

In the United States, typically 4–6% of the endowment's assets are spent every year to fund operations or capital spending. Any excess earnings are typically reinvested to augment the endowment and to compensate for inflation and recessions in future years.[[36]](#cite_note-Newsweek-36) This spending figure represents the proportion that historically could be spent without diminishing the principal amount of the endowment fund.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Criticism and reforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Criticism and reforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Fossil_Fuel_Divestment_Student_Protest_at_Tufts_University.jpg/250px-Fossil_Fuel_Divestment_Student_Protest_at_Tufts_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fossil_Fuel_Divestment_Student_Protest_at_Tufts_University.jpg)

Students at Tufts University in 2013 "marched forth on 4 March". The march was a divestment campaign with the goal of pressuring universities to [eliminate investments in fossil-fuel related ventures](/wiki/Fossil_fuel_divestment "Fossil fuel divestment").

### Donor intent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Donor intent")]

See also: [Donor intent](/wiki/Donor_intent "Donor intent") and [Community foundation](/wiki/Community_foundation "Community foundation")

The case of [Leona Helmsley](/wiki/Leona_Helmsley "Leona Helmsley") is often used to illustrate the downsides of the legal concept of donor intent as applied to endowments. In the 2000s, Helmsley bequested a multi-billion dollar trust to "the care and welfare of dogs".[[37]](#cite_note-Rosenwald-37) This trust was estimated at the time to total ten times more than the combined 2005 assets of all registered animal-related charities in the United States. A court later overturned her expressed intent that the entire trust be used for "the care and welfare of dogs", seemingly based on a technicality -- that intent was in a document separate from the actual trust and will documents.[[38]](#cite_note-38) It is not clear whether her intent would have been upheld if it had been fully and legally incorporated in those documents.

In 1914, Frederick Goff sought to eliminate the "dead hand" of organized philanthropy and so created [the Cleveland Foundation](/wiki/The_Cleveland_Foundation "The Cleveland Foundation"): the first [community foundation](/wiki/Community_foundation "Community foundation"). He created a corporately structured foundation that could utilize community gifts in a responsive and need-appropriate manner. Scrutiny and control resided in the "live hand" of the public as opposed to the "dead hand" of the founders of private foundations.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Economic downturn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Economic downturn")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(January 2026)* |

Research published in the [American Economic Review](/wiki/American_Economic_Review "American Economic Review") indicates that major academic endowments often act in times of economic downturn in a way opposite of the intention of the endowment. This behavior is referred to as endowment hoarding, reflecting the way that economic downturns often lead to endowments decreasing their payouts rather than increasing them to compensate for the downturn.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Large U.S.-based college and university endowments, which had posted large, highly publicized gains in the 1990s and 2000s, faced significant losses of principal in the [2008 economic downturn](/wiki/2008_economic_downturn "2008 economic downturn"). [The Harvard University endowment](/wiki/Harvard_University_endowment "Harvard University endowment"), which held $37 billion in June 2008, was reduced to $26 billion by mid-2009.[[41]](#cite_note-41) [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), the pioneer of an approach that involved investing heavily in alternative investments such as real estate and [private equity](/wiki/Private_equity "Private equity"), reported an endowment of $16 billion as of September 2009, a 30% annualized loss that was more than predicted in December 2008.[[42]](#cite_note-42) At [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University"), the endowment was reduced from $17 billion to $12 billion as of September 2009.[[43]](#cite_note-43) [Brown University](/wiki/Brown_University "Brown University")'s endowment fell 27 percent to $2.04 billion in the fiscal year that ended June 30, 2009.[[44]](#cite_note-44) [George Washington University](/wiki/George_Washington_University "George Washington University") lost 18% in that same fiscal year, down to $1.08 billion.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

In Canada, after the financial crisis in 2008, [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto") reported a loss of 31% ($545 million) of its previous year-end value in 2009. The loss is attributed to over-investment in hedge funds.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Endowment repatriation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Endowment repatriation")]

Critics like Justice Funders' Dana Kawaoka-Chen call for "redistributing all aspects of well-being, democratizing power, and shifting economic control to communities.".[[47]](#cite_note-47) Endowment repatriation refers to campaigns that acknowledge the history of human and natural resource exploitation that is inherent to many large private funds. Repatriation campaigns ask for private endowments to be returned to the control of the people and communities that have been most affected by labor and environmental exploitation and often offer ethical frameworks for discussing endowment governance and repatriation.[[48]](#cite_note-justicefunders.org-48)
[[49]](#cite_note-49)

> Many might say that, by definition, philanthropy is about redistributing resources. Yet to truly embody this principle, philanthropy must move far beyond the 5% payout requirements for grants and distribute ALL of its power and resources. This includes spending down one's endowment, investing in local and regional economic initiatives that build community wealth rather than investing in Wall Street, giving up decision-making power for grants, and, ultimately, turning over assets to community control.[[48]](#cite_note-justicefunders.org-48)

— Justice Funders

After the Heron Foundation's internal audit of its investments in 2011 uncovered an investment in a private prison that was directly contrary to the foundation's mission, they developed and then began to advocate for a four-part ethical framework to endowment investments conceptualized as Human Capital,
Natural Capital, Civic Capital, and Financial Capital.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Another example is the [Ford Foundation](/wiki/Ford_Foundation "Ford Foundation")'s co-founding of the independent [Native Arts and Culture Foundation](/w/index.php?title=Native_Arts_and_Culture_Foundation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Native Arts and Culture Foundation (page does not exist)") in 2007. The Ford Foundation provided a portion of the initial endowment after self-initiated research into the foundation's financial support of Native and Indigenous artists and communities. This results of this research indicated "the inadequacy of philanthropic support for Native arts and artists", related feedback from an unnamed Native leader that "[o]nce [big foundations] put the stuff in place for an Indian program, then it is not usually funded very well. It lasts as long as the program officer who
had an interest and then goes away" and recommended that an independent endowment be established and that "[n]ative leadership is crucial".[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Divestment campaigns and impact investing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Divestment campaigns and impact investing")]

See also: [Disinvestment](/wiki/Disinvestment "Disinvestment") and [Impact investing](/wiki/Impact_investing "Impact investing")

Another approach to reforming endowments is the use of [divestment](/wiki/Divestment "Divestment") campaigns to encourage endowments to not hold unethical investments. One of the earliest modern divestment campaigns was [Disinvestment from South Africa](/wiki/Disinvestment_from_South_Africa "Disinvestment from South Africa") which was used to protest apartheid policies. By the end of apartheid, more than 150 universities divested of South African investments, although it is not clear to what extent this campaign was responsible for ending the policy.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

A proactive version of divestment campaigns is [impact investing, or mission investing](/wiki/Impact_investing "Impact investing") which refers to investments "made into companies, organizations, and funds with the intention to generate a measurable, beneficial social or environmental impact alongside a financial return."[[53]](#cite_note-53) [Impact investments](/wiki/Impact_investment "Impact investment") provide capital to address social and environmental issues.

### Endowment taxes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_endowment&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Endowment taxes")]

Main article: [Endowment tax](/wiki/Endowment_tax "Endowment tax")

Generally, endowment taxes are the taxation of financial endowments that are otherwise not taxed due to their charitable, educational, or religious mission. Endowment taxes are sometimes enacted in response to criticisms that endowments are not operating as [nonprofit organizations](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization") or that they have served as [tax shelters](/wiki/Tax_shelter "Tax shelter"), or that they are [depriving local governments of essential property and other taxes](/wiki/Payment_in_lieu_of_taxes "Payment in lieu of taxes").[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)
